text: The laparoscopic camera provides a view from the left upper quadrant port, focusing on the right lower quadrant of the abdomen.

The surgeon identifies a gangrenous appendix walled off between the terminal ileum and the retroperitoneum, with lateral attachment of the terminal ileum over the appendix. The surgeon begins to divide the attachment, then carefully retracts the small bowel from the pelvis to avoid the right iliac vessels and ureter.

The camera view is directed deep into the pelvis, showing the sigmoid colon on the left side. Small bowel loops appear congealed together, with visible signs of purulent peritonitis in the surrounding area.

The surgeon continues dissection in the pelvic region, revealing loops of small bowel adhered to an abscess cavity. The loops are carefully lifted to fully expose the gangrenous appendix, ensuring critical structures are not compromised during the procedure.
The surgeon has unroofed the abscess cavity and is using blunt dissection to lift the small bowel away from the abscess cavity, moving it into the upper abdomen.

The camera focuses on the gangrenous appendix located in the center of the screen, with the tip of the appendix clearly visible. The surgeon examines a band of tissue, confirming the absence of the ureter or other critical structures nearby.
The surgeon prepares to divide the lateral attachment of the terminal ileum over the gangrenous appendix, ensuring careful separation from surrounding structures.

The surgeon lifts the small bowel, revealing the appendix completely free from adhesions. The appendix is manipulated to assess the base for potential closure options. The surgeon decides to approach through the mesentery to the appendix base, lifting the appendix tip from the right pelvic side wall.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the appendix, avoiding avulsion as it separates from the wall, revealing a potential necrotic back wall. The surgeon progresses towards the base of the appendix, ensuring the ileocecal valve is clear of the surgical field. A small nub is identified, and the surgeon assesses the base of the appendix for further action.
The surgeon identifies a one-centimeter stump of the appendix and prepares to close it off. The inflamed tissues are elevated, and a stapler is used to achieve a secure closure.
The surgeon removes the gangrenous appendix from the abdomen, followed by irrigation of the surgical site to clear any debris or residual fluids.
The surgeon irrigates the abdominal cavity with approximately two liters of saline, ensuring thorough removal of loculations and separation of interloop abscesses.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field to ensure there are no remaining loculated collections in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon assesses the retroperitoneum, noting it appears clean except for a fibrotic plaque. A drain is placed to mitigate the risk of a delayed abscess, and omentum is positioned in the pelvis.
